coredata.dc:title: Putrescine-functionalized carbon quantum dot (put-CQD) nanoparticle: A promising stress-protecting agent against cadmium stress in grapevine (Vitis vinifera cv. Sultana)

coredata.dc:description: Due to their sessile nature, plant cannot escape from stress factors in their growing environment, in either biotic or abiotic nature. Amid the abiotic stress factors; high levels of soil cadmium (Cd) impose heavy metal stress on plants, resulting in critical injuries and reduced agronomic performance. In order to buffer the adverse effects of Cd stress, novel nanoparticles (NP) have been applied and notable improvements have been reported. According to the literature, the protective roles of polyamines (e.g., Putrescine; Put) and carbon quantum dots (CQD) have been reported with respect to the plant productivity under either stress or non-stress conditions. Those reports led us to hypothesize that the conjugation of Put and CQD (Put-CQD NPs) might lead to further augmented performance of plants under stress and non-stress conditions. In this regard, we successfully synthesized a novel nanomaterial Put-CQD NPs. In this respect, Put (50 mg L−1), CQD (50 mg L−1) and Put-CQD NPs (25 and 50 mg L−1) were sprayed in ‘Sultana’ grapevines under Cd stress (10 mg kg−1). As expected, upon stress, Cd content in leaf and root tissues increased by 103.40% and 65.15%, respectively (p < 0.05). The high uptake and accumulation of Cd in plant tissues were manifested in significant alterations of physiological and biochemical attributes of the plant. Concerning stress markers, Cd stress caused increases in content of induced MDA, H2O2, and proline as well as electrolyte leakage rate. As expected, Cd stress caused critical reductions in fresh and dry leaf weight by 21.31% and 42.34%, respectively (p < 0.05). On the other hand, both Put-CQD NPs increased fresh and dry leaf weigh up to approximately 30%. The Cd-mediated disturbances in photosynthetic pigments and chlorophyll fluorescence were buffered with Put-CQD NPs. Of the defence system, enzymatic (SOD, APX, GP) as well as anthocyanin and phenolics were induced by both Cd stress and Put-CQD NPs (p < 0.05). On the other hand, Cd stress reduced content of polyamines (putrescine (Put), spermine (Spm) and spermidine (Spd) by 39.28%, 53.36%, and 39.26%, respectively (p < 0.05). However, the reduction levels were buffered by the treatments. Considering the effectiveness of both NP concentrations, the lower dose (25 mg L−1) could be considered as an optimal concentration. To our knowledge, this is the first report of its kind as a potential agent to reduce the adverse effects of Cd stress in grapevines.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to their sessile nature, plant cannot escape from stress factors in their growing environment, in either biotic or abiotic nature. Amid the abiotic stress factors; high levels of soil cadmium (Cd) impose heavy metal stress on plants, resulting in critical injuries and reduced agronomic performance. In order to buffer the adverse effects of Cd stress, novel nanoparticles (NP) have been applied and notable improvements have been reported. According to the literature, the protective roles of polyamines (e.g., Putrescine; Put) and carbon quantum dots (CQD) have been reported with respect to the plant productivity under either stress or non-stress conditions. Those reports led us to hypothesize that the conjugation of Put and CQD (Put-CQD NPs) might lead to further augmented performance of plants under stress and non-stress conditions. In this regard, we successfully synthesized a novel nanomaterial Put-CQD NPs. In this respect, Put (50 mg L−1), CQD (50 mg L−1) and Put-CQD NPs (25 and 50 mg L−1) were sprayed in ‘Sultana’ grapevines under Cd stress (10 mg kg−1). As expected, upon stress, Cd content in leaf and root tissues increased by 103.40% and 65.15%, respectively (p < 0.05). The high uptake and accumulation of Cd in plant tissues were manifested in significant alterations of physiological and biochemical attributes of the plant. Concerning stress markers, Cd stress caused increases in content of induced MDA, H2O2, and proline as well as electrolyte leakage rate. As expected, Cd stress caused critical reductions in fresh and dry leaf weight by 21.31% and 42.34%, respectively (p < 0.05). On the other hand, both Put-CQD NPs increased fresh and dry leaf weigh up to approximately 30%. The Cd-mediated disturbances in photosynthetic pigments and chlorophyll fluorescence were buffered with Put-CQD NPs. Of the defence system, enzymatic (SOD, APX, GP) as well as anthocyanin and phenolics were induced by both Cd stress and Put-CQD NPs (p < 0.05). On the other hand, Cd stress reduced content of polyamines (putrescine (Put), spermine (Spm) and spermidine (Spd) by 39.28%, 53.36%, and 39.26%, respectively (p < 0.05). However, the reduction levels were buffered by the treatments. Considering the effectiveness of both NP concentrations, the lower dose (25 mg L−1) could be considered as an optimal concentration. To our knowledge, this is the first report of its kind as a potential agent to reduce the adverse effects of Cd stress in grapevines.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cadmium (Cd) is a mobile and water-soluble heavy metal. Excessive accumulation of Cd in growing media causes drastic changes and damage to plant and human health (Azimi et al., 2021; Riaz et al., 2021). Cd is mainly found in soil and is transported to the plants via the roots. Once inside the plants with an excessive rate, reactive oxygen species (ROS) burst occurs in plants. The ROS burst critically causes damage to cellular components, such as lipids, proteins and DNA (Ali et al., 2018; Tajti et al., 2018; Hasanuzzaman et al., 2020; Zaid and Wani, 2019; Azimi et al., 2021; Riaz et al., 2021; L. Wang et al., 2021). With respect to critical modifications in plants, Cd negatively affects various central processes such as water and nutrient uptake, photosynthesis, membrane stability, enzymatic and non-enzymatic antioxidants, DNA structure and gene expression (Ali et al., 2014; Ismael et al., 2019; Azimi et al., 2021; Riaz et al., 2021; Zaid et al., 2020). The adverse effects are clearly manifested as decreased growth, reduced photosynthesis, decreased yields, and increased susceptibility to disease. Along with the Cd stress, interferences in uptake of some essential nutrients such as zinc and copper further exacerbate the negative effects of Cd stress (Kumar et al., 2015).

In this regard, plants have evolved a myriad of mechanisms to cope with high level of Cd content, having both enzymatic (i.e., SOD, CAT, and POD) and non-enzymatic antioxidant defence (i.e., flavonoids, polyphenols) mechanisms to protect themselves from the harmful effects of ROS produced during photosynthesis and stress conditions. These defense mechanisms act together and in concert to protect the plant from oxidative stress, which can damage to the cells and disrupt normal physiological processes (Yu et al., 2013; Mishra et al., 2014; Lukačová et al., 2013). In most cases, the activated defence system results in suspended growth and ultimately reduction in crop productivity (Figueroa-Macías et al., 2021). For that reason, a quite number of attempts have been done to reduce the effects of Cd stress, to improve plant growth and to understand mechanism of Cd stress on plant. For instance; uses of Cd-tolerant crops (Y. Wang et al., 2021), phytoremediation (Mahajan and Kaushal, 2018), addition of chelating agents (Teng et al., 2022; Chen et al., 2020), use of rhizosphere bacteria (Wang et al., 2019), application of humic acids (Farouk et al., 2011) or micronutrients (Azizi et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2020) are of the well-known conventional ways to reduce the effects of Cd.

However, a relatively new era of researches based on novel nanoengineered materials has gained a great attention in the recent in combating with the stress conditions (Ali et al., 2022). Nanoparticles (NPs) have been explored as a potential way to alleviate the negative effects of Cd stress in plants (Sharifi et al., 2021; Zaid et al., 2020; Azimi et al., 2021; Dai et al., 2021), being applied to the soil or directly the plants as seed priming or foliar. For instance; ZnO (Faizan et al., 2021a,b; Hussain et al., 2018; Ali et al., 2019), Fe2O3 (Rizwan et al., 2019a), SiO2 (Rizwan et al., 2019b), and graphene oxide (Gao et al., 2020a-b) NPs have been found to reduce the uptake of Cd in plants and to improve plant growth. NPs not only reduced Cd uptake but also improved the uptake of essential nutrients.

In addition, carbon quantum dot (CQD) nanoparticles (NPs) are a novel group of quantum dot (QD) NPs in which carbonic compounds contain oxygen (Lim et al., 2015; Gohari et al., 2021a). QD NPs are mostly used as the carrier, thanks to their very small size (2–10 nm), and for subcellular labelling and imaging through their distinctive fluorescent characteristics. QD NPs additionally act as semiconductors with quantization of energy and consequently exert remarkable biological activities (El-Esawi et al., 2020; Biju et al., 2010; Bai et al., 2019). CQD NPs are characterized by exclusive properties such as small size like other QDs (less than 10 nm), round shape, low or no toxicity, high water solubility, biocompatibility and biodegradability. These properties enable CQD NPs to be used for diverse functions (Gohari et al., 2021a). For instance, the positive effects of graphene QD NPs (Chakravarty et al., 2015) and CQD NPs (Li et al., 2018; Gohari et al., 2021a, 2021b) on plant metabolism were clearly reported. Specifically, CQD NPs functioning as a carrier gives us an opportunity to functionalize them with various molecules with stress-protective effects as previously reported for Put (Gohari et al., 2021a) and proline (Gohari et al., 2021b).

Polyamines (PAs) are a class of low molecular weight and positively-charged compound with two or more primary amino groups, being widely distributed in plants. The most common PAs in plants are putrescine (Put), spermidine (Spd) and spermine (Spm). Of the PAs, Put has been reported to have modulating roles in plants suffering from stress. The function of Put under stress conditions could be related to its vital roles in improving photosynthetic proficiency, protecting biological membranes and cellular structures, activating antioxidant machinery, and up-regulating stress-related genes (Alcázar et al., 2010; Sequera-Mutiozabal et al., 2017; Alcázar et al., 2020; Gohari et al., 2021a). Importantly, Put is a metal chelator with important functions in combating heavy metal toxicity (Pál et al., 2017; 2018), primarily via the activation of the antioxidant system (Tang et al., 2005; Zhao and Yang, 2008a,b; Pál et al., 2018) as reported for cadmium (Cd) (Kumar et al., 2012; Pál et al., 2015; Tajti et al., 2018).

Grapevine (Vitis vinifera L.) is of the significant fruit crops, being characterized by two productive organs (fruit berry and seeds). Those organs contain a large quantity of soluble flavonoids which contribute to the nutraceutical potential of grapevines (Braidot et al., 2008). As of the other plant species, grapevines are also under pressure of an array of biotic and abiotic stress factors, in general (Palliotti et al., 2014; Alagić et al., 2015; Karimi et al., 2020) and Cd stress, in particular (Li et al., 2022; Shao et al., 2010; Howladar et al., 2018). In order to reduce the negative effects of stress factors, and then to ensure the plants to reach to the fruit and seeds, novel approaches are need of hour. For that reason, we herein addressed the current work on potential uses of newly synthesized nanoparticles in alleviating the negative effects of Cd.

Specifically, CQD NPs functioning as a carrier gives us an opportunity to functionalize them with various molecules with stress-protective effects as previously reported for Put (Gohari et al., 2021a) and proline (Gohari et al., 2021b). Putrescine-functionalized carbon quantum dot nanoparticles (Put-CQD NPs) were previously synthesized and successfully applied as the priming agent on grapevines to minimize the effects of salinity stress (Gohari et al., 2021a). Put-CQD NPs improved plant morphological, physiological and tolerance-related parameters. The protective function of these NPs could be attributed to the synergistic effects of Put and CQD in the combined nano-form, and especially through enhanced entry and translocation of Put into cells through the carrier function of CQDs (Gohari et al., 2021a). Considering the individual roles of Put and CQD-NPs and their conjugated form in plants, we hypothesized that Put-CQD NPs could reduce and improve growth of grapevines subjected to Cd stress by modifying diverse biochemical phenomena such as enhancing antioxidant capacity, buffering stress indexes and restoring metabolic activities. To test this hypothesis, the potential protective roles of Put-CQD NPs against Cd stress were investigated in Vitis vinifera cv. Sultana by assaying an array of agronomic, physiological and biochemical traits.

Putrescine functionalized carbon quantum dot nanoparticles (Put-CQDs NPs) were synthesized based on our previous report (Gohari et al., 2021a). Briefly, 1 g Put and 4 g citric acid were mixed and dissolved in 10 ml deionized water to obtain a clear solution. Then, the mixture was transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave chamber and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. Finally, the pH of the solution was adjusted to 7.0 by adding NaOH before use and characterization. It should be highlighted that bare CQDs were synthesized as control. The structure composition and morphology of Synthesized Put-CQD NPs were characterized using FTIR and TEM analysis.

The study was carried out as a factorial experiment using a completely randomized design with four biological replications at research greenhouses of the Faculty of Agriculture, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran. Grapevine (Vitis vinifera L.) cv. Sultana (i.e. three-year-old cuttings) were planted in 7-liter (25*19*20 cm) pots containing a mixture of cadmium chloride (CdCl2, Merck)-contaminated research farm soil (as cadmium (Cd) source; (0 and 10 mg kg−1)) and sand (3:1 ratio) (Azimi et al., 2021). The physico-chemical properties of the experimental soils were as follows: 23.14% clay, 42.16% silt and 34.7% sand, a loamy texture, pH:7.35, electrical conductivity of 3.1 (dS/m), organic matter of 2.58%, organic carbon of 2.01%, and calcium carbonate of 13.27%. Each pot contained a cutting and was irrigated every three days with tap water up to sampling. At eight-leaf stage, Put (50 mg L−1), carbon quantum dot (CQD; 50 mg L−1) and Put carbon quantum dot nanoparticles (Put-CQD NPs; 25 and 50 mg L−1), each at four replications, were sprayed on grapevine leaves as stress protective treatments four times with five days interval. Control plants were planted in the uncontaminated research farms soil and sand (3:1 ratio) and irrigated in the same manner until the sampling and treated with any treatments. Regarding preparation and synthesis of Put-CQD NPs, the same experimental workflows were done and the same characterization results were noted (Gohari et al., 2021a). All measurements were performed four weeks after the application of the last treatments using fully expanded leaves. All measurements were performed using four replicates.

For estimation of leaf fresh weight (FW) and dry weight (DW), three fully-developed leaf samples from 3rd or 4th nodes were randomly selected. After recording FW, the leaf samples were dried at 70 °C for 72 h before DW measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photosynthetic pigments (chlorophyll a, b and carotenoids) and chlorophyll fluorescence parameters (Fv/Fm, Y (NO) and Y (II))
ce:section.ce:para: Randomly selected leaves from 2nd or 3rd nodes were used for analyses. For extraction of pigments, 0.5 g of leaf samples were incubated in 10 ml acetone (3% v/v). The extraction was followed by centrifuging the samples (4000 rpm, 10 min). The absorbances of the supernatant were recorded at 663 nm, 645 nm and 470 nm using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-1800 Shimadzu, Japan). The concentration of Chl a, Chl b and carotenoids were calculated according to Lichtenthaler (1987). Chlorophyll fluorescence parameters including Fv/Fm, Y (NO) and Y (II) were estimated using a dual-pam-100 chlorophyll fluorometer (Heinz Walz, Effeltrich, Germany) after plant adaption in the dark (Maxwell and Johnson, 2000).

After washing leaf and root samples with deionized water, tissues were kept in oven (65 °C, 48 h) and were later powdered homogeneously. Then, the powdered samples (1 g) were digested with HNO3/HClO4 (10 mL) at 100 °C and lastly kept in furnace (550 °C, 5 h) to obtain their ash. The gained ashes were reached to the final volume (50 mL) by addition of distilled water in volumetric flask, after dissolving with HCl (2 N) and filtering (Whatman filter paper). Finally, Cd content was quantified using Atomic absorption spectrometer (Model CTA 3000, ChemTech, UK) (Azizi et al., 2020).

MDA content was quantified according to the method of Stewart and Bewley (1980). Briefly, leaf samples (0.1 g) were homogenized with acetic acid (2.5 mL; 10% w/v) and then centrifuged (15000 rpm, 20 min). Then, 2.5 ml of homogenized extracts and thiobarbituric acid (0.5% w/v) in trichloroacetic acid (TCA) (20%) were transferred to the test tube (96 °C, 30 min) and incubated at 0 °C (5 min) and then after centrifuged (10000 rpm, 5 min). Finally, the absorbance of each sample was recorded at 532 and 600 nm by UV-spectrophotometer (UV-1800 Shimadzu, Japan). Absorbances were converted to MDA content using the extinction coefficient of 155 mM−1 cm−1.

For quantification of H2O2 content, the method proposed by Sinha et al. (2005) was used. Briefly, leaf samples (0.2 g) were mixed with trichloroacetic acid (5 mL, 0.1% w/v) in an ice bath and centrifuging (12000 rpm, 15 min), then potassium phosphate buffer (0.5 mL, pH 6.8, 10 mM) and potassium iodide (1 mL, 1 M) were added to the supernatant (0.5 mL). Finally, the absorbance was recorded at 390 nm and through standard calibration curve formerly attained by different H2O2 concentrations, the content was calculated.

Proline content was measured according to Bates et al. (1973) with some modifications. Briefly, the leaf samples (0.5 g) were homogenized with aqueous sulfosalicylic acid (10 mL, 3%) and then the extracts were centrifuged (1000 rpm, 4 °C). Then the supernatants were mixed with ninhydrin acid and glacial acetic acid (1:1:1); incubated (100 °C, 1 h) and placed in an ice bath, respectively. Finally, after addition of toluene (4 mL) to the mixture, the absorbances of the samples were recorded at 520 nm.

For anthocyanin content, leaf samples (0.1 g) were firstly extracted using 5 ml of acidified methanol (HCl–methanol:1:99) by incubation at dark for 24 h. The homogenates were then centrifuged (10000 rpm, 10 min). Absorbances of the supernatants were recorded at 550 nm (Wagner, 1979).

For phenolic content, 0.1 g of leaf samples were digested using 5 ml of ethanol (95%). The homogenates were centrifuged (12000 rpm, 10 min). Then, the supernatant (1 ml) was mixed with ethanol (1 ml, 95%) and distilled water (3 ml). The mixture was vortexed for a few seconds. To the mixture, Folin-Ciocalteu solution (0.5 mL, 50%) and sodium bicarbonate (1 mL, 5%) were added and again incubated at dark for 1 h. Finally, the absorbances of the samples were recorded at 725 nm. Total phenolic content was quantified using the standard curve obtained by different concentrations of gallic acid (Xu et al., 2010).

After digesting leaf samples (0.5 g) with potassium phosphate buffer (pH 6.8, 100 mM) containing polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) and EDTA and centrifuging (6000 rpm, 20 min, 4 °C), the collected supernatants were used to evaluate ascorbate peroxidase (APX) (Nakano and Asada, 1981), superoxide dismutase (SOD) (Flohe and Günzler, 1984), and guaiacol peroxidase (GP) (Tang and Newton, 2005) enzymes activities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Polyamine (PA) extraction and HPLC analysis of free PAs
ce:section.ce:para: After homogenizing leaf samples (0.1 g) with cold perchloric acid (1.5 mL, 5% (v/v)) and keeping at 4 °C (1 h), the mixtures were centrifuged (15000 g, 40 min) and the supernatants, including the free polyamine fraction, was kept for further analysis. Standards and plant extracts were then subjected to benzoylation reaction. Briefly, after mixing NaOH (1 mL, 2 N) with plant extract (500 mL), benzoyl chloride (10 mL) was added to the mixture and vortexed (30 s) and incubated (room temperature, 20 min) and finally saturated NaCl (2 mL) was added. Benzoyl PAs were extracted in 2 mL diethyl ether and 1 mL of the ether phase was collected, evaporated to dryness, and re-dissolved in 200 mL methanol. In a similar way, the standards of PAs were treated with the cell extract in the reaction mixture (Flores and Galston, 1982).

HPLC system (Waters Series HPLC, Milford, MA, USA) was used to separate the benzoyl derivatives. In this regard, 20 mL of each benzoyl PA was injected into a 20-mL loop, loaded onto a C18 reverse-phase column (25 cm, 2.1 mm, 5 mm; Supelco Analytical, Bellefonte, PA, USA) at room temperature. Samples were eluted from the column in a gradient program at a flow rate of 0.2 mL/min, according to (Kotzabasis et al., 2012), with some modifications. Polyamine peaks were detected with a UV detector at 254 nm. Different concentrations of Put, Spd, and Spm standards (Sigma Chemical Co.) were used for providing the standard curves.

Data analysis was performed by SAS software (SAS Institute Inc., ver. 9.1, Cary, NC, USA) through the means of the applied replications for each treatment via Duncan's multiple range test (p < 0.05). In order to correlate, visualize and classify the dependent (investigated parameters) and independent variable (treatments), heat-map clustering (ClustVis), correlation analysis (JASP) and principal component analysis were performed (JAMOVI). Furthermore, the linkages of the independent variables according to their performances with respect to the dependent variables were revealed with Network Plot Analysis (PAST Software).

Put-CQDs were fabricated by one-pot and easy hydrothermal method. FTIR and TEM analysis revealed the successful formation of Put-CQDs. As it could be seen from Fig. 1 A, the stretching vibrations of O–H and C–H occurred at 3480 and 2985 cm−1. The characteristic bands related to bending vibration N–H and C–NH–C could be detected at 1558 and 1389 cm−1, respectively. In addition, the existence of bands around 1665 and 1033 cm−1 confirmed the successful formation of CO and C–N bonds, respectively. Uniform dispersion of nanoparticles with the size of 3–5 nm could be seen from TEM image in Fig. 2 B.

Cd stress critically reduced leaf FW and DW by 21.31% and 42.34%, respectively (p < 0.05). Concerning solo applications of Put and CQD NPs under Cd-stress free conditions, Put 50 mg L−1 increased FW by 8.09% and CQD NPs 50 mg L−1 did not contribute to leaf FW (Fig. 2 A). On the other hand, Put-CQD NPs 25 mg L−1 and 50 mg L−1 also increased leaf FW by 28.26% and 31.37%, respectively. Regarding Cd-stress submitted grapevine, all treatments, except CQDs NPs 50 mg L−1 + Cd, buffered and reduced the effects of stress. Being in parallel with the values of leaf FW, individual or conjugated forms of Put or CQD increased leaf DW with a range between 7.03% and 38.95% (Fig. 2 B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chl a, b and carotenoids and chlorophyll fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: Chl a, b and carotenoid contents were significantly reduced by 37.19%, 30.45% and 45.77%, respectively in plants suffering from Cd stress (p < 0.05). Put, CQDs and their conjugation significantly increased content of Chl-a with a rate of 32.44% (CDQ NPs 50 mg L−1) to 86.42% (Put- CDQ NPs 50 mg L−1) in comparison to control. On the other hand, adverse effects of Cd were partially buffered and alleviated by the treatment, in comparison to the Cd-stress submitted plants (Fig. 3 A). Being very similar to Chl-a, Put, CQDs and their conjugation increased Chl b and carotenoid in control or Cd-stressed plants (Fig. 3 B, C).

Cd stress caused significant reductions in Fv/Fm (−33.25%) and Y (II) (−47.28%) and increases in Y (NO) (71.84%). Fv/Fm was positively affected by Put and Put-CQD NPs (25 and 50 mg L−1) under control and Cd stress conditions (Fig. 4 A). Application of all treatments increased Y (II) under control condition; Put and both concentrations of Put-CQD NPs were effective treatments in increasing Y (II) under Cd stress condition (Fig. 4 B). Considering Y (NO), 25 mg L-1 Put-CQD NPs was the only treatment with promising effect under control condition where all applied treatments caused positive impacts under Cd stress condition (Fig. 4 C).

High level Cd in soil increased leaf Cd and root Cd content by 103.40% and 65.15%, respectively (p < 0.05). In comparison to the control, a reduction rate of 4.65% (Put-CQD NPs 25 mg L−1) to 34.32% (Put-CQD NPs 50 mg L−1) was observed in leaf Cd content under non-stress conditions. On the other hand, treatment reduced Cd content in leaf by 47.04%, in comparison to Cd-stressed plants (Fig. 5 A). Similar to Cd accumulation behaviour of leaf, all treatments decreased Cd content in root, in either control or Cd-stress submitted plants (Fig. 5 B).

Cd stress increased MDA and H2O2 content by 94.45% and 41.33%, respectively (p < 0.05). In comparison to control, a reduction rate as 6.00%–37.70% was observed by the treatments. However, Put and 25 mg L−1 Put-CQD NP treatment reduced MDA content under stress-free condition, whereas Put and both concentrations of the NPs lowered MDA under Cd stress (Fig. 6 A). H2O2 content was lessened by the application of Put-CQD NPs (25 and 50 mg L−1) treatments under control and Cd stress conditions (Fig. 6 B).

Cd stress increased EL and proline content by 17.50% and 87.68%, respectively (p < 0.05). In non-stress conditions, EL rate decreased with a rate as 7.03% (CQD NP 50 mg L−1) to 26.36% (Put-CQD NP 50 mg L−1). In Cd-stressed plants, treatments decreased EL values between 6.98% (CQDs NPs 50 mg L−1 + Cd) and 32.37% (Put-CQD NP 50 mg L−1) (Fig. 7 A). Concerning proline content, not only Cd stress but also all treatments resulted in high levels of proline up to 173.70% (Put-CQD NP 50 mg L−1) (Fig. 7 B).

Cd stress increased the total anthocyanin and phenolic content by 75.30% and 62.98%, respectively (p < 0.05). Treatments increased total anthocyanin content in either control or Cd-stressed plants, reaching a rate of 229.91% with Put-CQD NP 50 mg L−1 (Fig. 8 A). Similarly, total phenolic content also increased with all treatments, exhibiting a 214.84% increase with Put-CQD NP 25 mg L−1 (Fig. 8 B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: APX, SOD and GP antioxidant enzymes activities
ce:section.ce:para: Cd stress caused significant increases in activities of APX, SOD and GP by 34.77%, 24.37% and 36.40%, respectively (p < 0.05). Concerning APX activity, all treatments, except CQD NP 50 mg L−1, further increased activity and reached to 148.13% with Put-CQD NP 50 mg L−1 + Cd (Fig. 9 A). as the case of APX, SOD and GP activities also increased with the treatments, reaching to 75.16% (Put-CQD NP 25 mg L−1 + Cd) and 148.31% (Put-CQD NP 50 mg L−1 + Cd), respectively (Fig. 9 B, C). The highest enzymes activities were mostly recorded at Put-CQD NPs treatments.

The content of Put, Spm and Spd decreased under Cd stress by 39.28%, 53.36%, and 39.26%, respectively (p < 0.05). In Cd-stressed or control, Put and Put-CQD NPs (at both concentrations) increased content of Put and Spd (Fig. 10 A, B). Put and Put-CQD NPs enhanced Spm values under control condition while all treatments had promising effects under Cd stress (Fig. 10 C). In all, Put and Put-CQD NPs at 50 mg L−1 resulted in the upper Put, Spd and Spm content under both Cd stress and non-stress conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: An overall assessment by multivariate analyses
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to the one-way anova analysis, the physiological and biochemical data of the study were subjected to an array of statistical analysis, viz. heatmap clustering, correlation, principal component analysis (PCA) and network plot analysis in order to reduce the dimension, correlate, visualize and clarify the estimated parameters corresponding to the treatments. In the case of high number of dependent and independent variables, such multivariate statistical analysis is quite widely employed. In this regard, we firstly constructed a heat map (Fig. 11 A). Accordingly, Cd-stress submitted and non-stress conditions (all experimental groups without Cd stress) were clearly separated into the two distinct major clusters. The first cluster included “control, Put, CQD, and Put-CQD NPs”, corresponding to the non-stress conditions. The relevant cluster was positively correlated with Fv/Fm, Y (II), Chl a, Chl b, Put, Spd, Spm, carotenoid, leaf FW, and leaf DW. On the other hand, the second cluster involved “Cd-stress and its interaction with Put, CQD and Put-CQD NPs”, corresponding to the Cd-stress submitted groups. This cluster was positively associated with “stress indicators, antioxidant components and Cd-content of plants”. Those findings suggest that high Cd content in growing media is a clear discriminative and predictor factor with respect to the current estimated parameters. Also, correlation analysis clearly proves the negative correlations between stress and non-stress conditions (Fig. 11 B).

We furthermore performed PCA to determine the explained ratio of variance of the parameters. In this regard, all parameters and treatments were scattered and discriminated on a biplot pair (Fig. 12 A). Accordingly, two principal components with Eigenvalues >1.0, accounted for 91.3% of the variability of the original data. Such a high explained variance regarding our experimental findings suggest PCA might be a crucial predictor in assessment and discriminating the exogenous treatments. The first principal component (PC1; Eigen value: 13.90) accounted 63.2% of total variations, exhibiting positive correlations with Chl a-b, PAs, leaf FW and DW. On the other hand, in the second principal component (PC2; Eigen value: 6.18) accounting 28.11% of total variation; stress linked indexes, antioxidant system components and Cd-content of the samples had higher Eigenvectors.

We furthermore performed a network-plot analysis to reveal the linkage of individual treatments (Cd-stress, control, Put, CQD, Put-CQD NPs and concentrations of NPs) based on their performance on the physiological and biochemical parameters estimated along with the current study (Fig. 12 B). The plot includes nodes via line and the depth of the lines, which reveal the relation between the experimental groups. The thinner/lighter line expresses the weaker relation whilst the thicker line displays the strong relations with each other. According to the network plot analysis, as the case of Cd-stress, which is the main concern of the study; Cd-stressed treatments were clearly separated from other groups, except CQD NPs 50 mg L−1 + Cd and Put 50 mg L−1 + Cd. Considering the relations of Cd-stress, “Cd-stress group” has a strong similar index with” CQD NPs 50 mg L−1 + Cd”, whilst “Cd-stress group” exhibited a low similar index with “Put 50 mg L−1 + Cd” according to the network and thickness of the lines. Significantly, conjugated forms of Put with CQD NPs were quite effective and separated from Cd stress groups without any linkages according to plot analysis. Also, the concentration of Put-CQD NPs were also similar regarding their performances on the estimated attributes. Those findings might strongly suggest that Put-CQD NPs are crucial and predictor factor in combating with Cd-stress, as the case of grapevine plants. However, the concentrations of Put-CQD NPs were not discriminative factors. Furthermore, being in consistent with analysis of heat map clustering, correlation and principal component; treatments of Put, CQD and Put-CQD NPs without Cd-stress were closely linked to each other, whereas interaction of Put, CQD and Put-CQD NPs with Cd stress were also scattered close to each other.

Cadmium (Cd) is of the significant metals owing to its wide industrial uses. However, its excessive accumulation in the environment makes it most dangerous metal, resulting in Cd stress for plants (Azevedo et al., 2012). As of the other stress factors, Cd stress limits the growth and productivity of plants (El Rasafi et al., 2022; Shanmugaraj et al., 2019). After a basic search on Web of Science using the terms “cadmium stress” on 24th January 2023; 3721 results from Web of Science Core Collection were recorded in “Plant Science” category during years of 1986–2023. The publications during 2018–2022 constituted approximately 32.36% (N = 1204) of the published documents, suggesting there is an increasing concern linked to Cd pollution and its potential risk on ecosystem components. In order to combat with the negative effects of Cd or reduce the uptake of Cd from soil to plant system, polyamines (PAs) (Rady and Hemida, 2015; Wen et al., 2011; Zhao and Yang, 2008a,b) and carbon quantum dots (Li et al., 2019) have been assayed and promising outcomes were reported with respect to reduced Cd uptake, plant growth and productivity. Those reports pioneered us to hypothesize that the conjugation form of putrescine and carbon quantum dots nanoparticles (Put-CQD NPs) could lead to further augmented performance of plants under control and Cd-stress conditions. In this regard, we successfully synthesized and fabricated Put-CQDs by one-pot and hydrothermal method. The potential effects of Put-CQDs on grapevines suffering from Cd stress were investigated through an array of physiological and biochemical attributes.

As expected, fresh and dry leaf weight were critically reduced in Cd-stress submitted grapevines. Those findings are consistent with the previous reports indicating that Cd stress caused dramatic decreases in plant growth parameters (e.g., Faizan et al., 2021a,b; Azimi et al., 2021; Irshad et al., 2021), by interrupting uptake and transport of water and nutrients, photosynthesis, respiration (Ismael et al., 2019; Haider et al., 2021), cell division and expansion, as well as carbohydrate synthesis (Hussain et al., 2020). Concerning exogenous treatments, Put has positively contributed to the leaf FW and DW and other agronomic traits under stress conditions (Saleethong et al., 2011; Zhang et al., 2014; Khoshbakht et al., 2017; Gohari et al., 2021a). The improvements have been attributed to the roles of Put in cell growth and cell division, modulation of ion metabolism. For that reason, the improvements noted in leaf FW and DW of grapevine might be linked to Put-CQD NPs-mediated increases in water and nutrient uptake and improvements in cell division as well as enlargement due to stronger influence of Put in the NPs form and using CQD as the carrier, as the case reported in our previous findings (Gohari et al., 2021a, 2021b). In addition to our previous reports (Gohari et al., 2021a, 2021b), Li et al. (2018) also presented encouraging effects of CQD in FW and DW of rice plants under salt stress due to improved nutrient uptake and antioxidant capacity.

We also observed critical reductions in content of Chl a, b and carotenoids in Cd-stress submitted grapevines. Such a critical decline might be explained with Cd-mediated reduction in Fe absorption, a critical element for critical element for the synthesis of the photosynthetic pigments and photosynthesis process and apparatus (Azimi et al., 2021; Haider et al., 2021) and production and accumulation of ROS (e.g., Muradoglu et al., 2015; Hussain et al., 2020; Azimi et al., 2021). Concerning chlorophyll fluorescence, Fv/Fm, Y (NO) and Y (II) were critically affected by Cd stress, as the case reported by Azimi et al. (2021). Over-replacement of magnesium (Mg) by Cd in the Chl molecules disrupts photosynthesis, dissipates energy and accumulates ROS and H2O2 (Andresen et al., 2016). However, Cd-stress induced decreases in Chl content and Fv/Fm were mostly reversed by PAs including Put and Spd (Tajti et al., 2018). As of the significant functions, PAs modulate stomatal closure via H2O2 production and interaction with nitric oxide (NO) signalling, Ca2+ and abscisic acid (ABA) (Alcázar et al., 2020). Concerning the photosynthesis machine of the plants, PAs exert significant effects on the thylakoid's membranes, the PSII, light-harvesting complex II, photosynthetic apparatus and protect the pigments by trapping Cd-induced ROS. For instance, Put enhanced the content of photosynthetic pigments under Cd stress condition through Chl synthesis, stomatal conductance and photosynthetic area (Nahar et al., 2016; Benavides et al., 2018). In addition, the affirmative effects of Put on photosynthetic proficiency and Chl could be explained with decreases in lipid peroxidation and ROS accumulation, increases in compatible osmolytes, activation of antioxidant system and photosynthesis-involved proteins (Gohari et al., 2021a).

Regarding uptake of Cd to the plant organs, we observed increases in Cd content in leaf and roots, being consistent with former reports (Wu et al., 2016; Ji et al., 2017; Tajti et al., 2018; Azimi et al., 2021). The increases in Cd content of plant organs are expectable due to its mobile and water-soluble nature and its entrance via membrane proteins and channels involved in absorption of nutrients (Haider et al., 2021). However, content of Cd might be buffered by CS-Se NPs (Azimi et al., 2021) and Put application (Nahar et al., 2016) under Cd stress.

Put action as a metal chelating material might be the main reason for its effect at buffering Cd content in plant tissues (Pál et al., 2017,2018), which in turn can lessen the negative effects of Cd in plants (Kumar et al., 2012). Furthermore, Put might prevent Cd stress through declining its active chemical form in soil, absorption and transfer in plant and ratio in the cell wall and stimulating its vacuole compartmentalization (Zhu et al., 2018). Protective influence of Put additionally might be attributed to its effect on changes in the composition of the cell wall that enable to lower binding of the heavy metals (e.g., Cd) (Zhu et al., 2018). The confirmed effects of Put next to its enriched properties in the nano-form containing the carrier could be presented as the prospective clarifications for Put-CQD NPs impacts on declining Cd content of root and leaf of grapevine under Cd stress.

Of the universal stress indicators, MDA and H2O2 in grapevine leaves increased by Cd stress. The higher levels of MDA, in comparison to the control, are considered as signs of injuries to membranes and lipids (Muradoglu et al., 2015; Gohari et al., 2021c, d). Similarly, high H2O2 has deleterious effects in biological membranes and some physiological processes through inducing oxidative stress (Azimi et al., 2021). As in the case of MDA, higher H2O2 values were also observed in plants exposed to Cd-stress (Andresen et al., 2016; Wu et al., 2016; Azimi et al., 2021), probably due to disorders in electron transference resulting in interrupted photosynthesis and respiration (Andresen et al., 2016; Azimi et al., 2021) and Cd interaction with antioxidant molecules that then caused lipid peroxidation and MDA enhancement (Muradoglu et al., 2015). It is known that Cd stress causes a cascade of injuries coupled with increases in H2O2, damage to cell membranes occurs and finally MDA increases (Hussain et al., 2020). Concerning the applications to buffer the stress-inducing indexes such as H2O2, MDA; CS-Se NPs (Azimi et al., 2021) and Graphene QD (Feng et al., 2019) were found to be effective in maintaining the levels of MDA and H2O2. Owing to the chemical structure, Put has a polycationic nature that ensures remarkable functions in maintaining cell ion balance, preserving DNA, RNA, proteins or membrane lipids by binding to them and lately, inhibiting lipid peroxidation and ROS accumulation by its antioxidant properties (Alcázar et al., 2020). Put activates stress protective pathways mediated by ROS, as well (Alcázar et al., 2010; 2020).

The stability and integrity of cell membranes have essential roles in plants protection against stress conditions. As a result, any damage to the membranes leads to increase in EL rate (Ganjavi et al., 2021; Hussain et al., 2013; Azizi et al., 2020; Azimi et al., 2021). Of the NPs employed, CS-Se NPs application decreased EL (enhanced membrane stability index; MSI) values under Cd stress (Azimi et al., 2021). Proline (50 and 100 mg L-1), CQD and proline-CQD NPs (both at 50 mg L-1) also critically reduced EL in grapevine under control and salinity (Gohari et al., 2021b). Lower rates of EL were recorded in Put, CQD- and Put-CQD NPs-treated plant under control and salinity (Gohari et al., 2021a). The improvement might be explained with the potential functions of Put in stabilizing biological membranes (Alcázar et al., 2010; Zhang et al., 2014; Sequera-Mutiozabal et al., 2017; Gohari et al., 2021a). In particular, direct binding of Put to negatively charged membrane phospholipid head groups results in preserving membrane functions and integrity under stress conditions (Gohari et al., 2021a). In this regard, the membrane stability roles of Put were reported for a quite number of species exposed to Cd stress (Yang et al., 2010; Nahar et al., 2016; Benavides et al., 2018; Shah et al., 2020).

High proline content was noted with the treatments and Cd stress, enhancing plant tolerance to stress conditions through acting as antioxidant osmolyte and osmotic pressure regulator (Alcázar et al., 2020; Azimi et al., 2021). The link between proline and metal ions results in augmented plant tolerance against stress damage. That can be explained with positive roles of proline associated with the metal-detoxification capacity of intracellular antioxidant enzymes and neutralizes heavy metal-induced ROS. Also, proline counteracts heavy metal toxicity through enhancing antioxidant enzymes activities, conserving cellular redox homeostasis, reconstructing chl, modulating intracellular pH and chelating metals (Emamverdian et al., 2015). Higher proline content was also observed for other plant species under Cd toxicity (John et al., 2008; Azizi et al., 2020; Azimi et al., 2021) to neutralize ROS (Hussain et al., 2020), to adjust osmotic pressure, to preserve cell membranes and to provide cell energy and nitrogen (John et al., 2008). Similarly, CS-Se NPs enhanced proline under control and Cd stress (Azimi et al., 2021). QD application enhanced proline content (Feng et al., 2019). Proline content of grapevine increased after proline (50 and 100 mg L-1), CQD and proline-CQD NPs (both at 50 mg L-1) under control and proline and proline-CQD NPs (both at 50 and 100 mg L-1) under salinity (Gohari et al., 2021b). Put plays role in osmolyte accumulation (Khoshbakht et al., 2017; Alcázar et al., 2020; Li et al., 2016; Tanou et al., 2014) and was also provided in the report of Gohari et al. (2021a) stating that Put and Put-CQD NPs application significantly increased proline content. Individual uses of Put and Spd also induced accumulation of proline in Cd-stress submitted plant (Tajti et al., 2018). Similar findings were reported after Put applications on plants under Cd stress (Nahar et al., 2016; Shah et al., 2020).

Phenolic compounds and their derivatives like anthocyanins, as non-enzymatic antioxidants, neutralize ROS, H2O2 and free radicals. Accordingly, their higher content under stress conditions are in parallel to their action in protecting plant cells (Ganjavi et al., 2021; Gohari et al., 2021a, 2021c; Emamverdian et al., 2015). Being consistent with the present findings, Cd stress also increased phenolic and anthocyanin content and the increases were likely linked to neutralize ROS (Hussain et al., 2020; Tarrahi et al., 2019; Azimi et al., 2021). In addition, QD increased phenolics in dose-dependent manner, the NPs higher concentrations led to the lower content (Feng et al., 2019). Put and Put-CQD NPs enhanced phenols under control condition and CQD and Put-CQD NPs had similar positive effect under salinity condition (Gohari et al., 2021a). Overall, reports revealed that Put might increase non-enzymatic antioxidants, in general (e.g., phenolics, anthocyanin) (Saleethong et al., 2011; Zhang et al., 2014; Gohari et al., 2021a) under Cd stress, in particular (Hsu and Kao, 2008; Nahar et al., 2016) by inducing the genes responsible for biosynthesis of enzymatic and non-enzymatic antioxidants in plants (He et al., 2008). Overall, we can suggest that Put-CQD NPs treated plants with elevated endogenous content of anthocyanin and phenolics might retain the antioxidant system of plants.

Cd-mediated generation and accumulation of ROS damage to plant cells due to disruption in electron transport chain and antioxidant system which could be neutralized by antioxidant enzymes (e.g., CAT, SOD, APX, GP) (Muradoglu et al., 2015; Azimi et al., 2021). However, the impacts of Cd stress on the activities of enzymes might vary according to the stress severity and time and plant species (Ji et al., 2017; Faizan et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2020; Haider et al., 2021). Cd stress enhanced SOD, CAT and APX (Muradoglu et al., 2015; Wu et al., 2016), APX and GP (Tajti et al., 2018), SOD and CAT (Tarrahi et al., 2019) and APX, SOD, CAT and GP (Azimi et al., 2021), leading to greater resistance most likely via enhanced glutathione-ascorbate cycle activity (Wu et al., 2016). CS-Se NPs enhanced CAT, GP, APX and SOD activities under control and Cd toxicity conditions (Azimi et al., 2021). Feng et al. (2019) reported QD dose-dependent effect on CAT activity. Proline, CQD and proline-CQD NPs (all at 50 and 100 mg L−1 concentrations) generally caused higher APX, CAT, SOD and GP under control and salinity conditions (Gohari et al., 2021b). In the same way, Put, CQD and Put-CQD NPs mostly enhanced CAT, GP, APX and SOD activities under control and salinity conditions (Gohari et al., 2021a) due to Put function in signalling, stress protection (Saleethong et al., 2011; Zhang et al., 2014; Gohari et al., 2021a), cell survival (Li et al., 2016) and the expression of genes encoding antioxidant enzymes. Put enhanced SOD and CAT enzymes activities and decreased adverse ROS effects and membrane injuries (Khoshbakht et al., 2017). Moreover, PAs reversed heavy metal-induced oxidative stress primarily via the activation of the antioxidant system (Tang et al., 2005; Zhao and Yang, 2008a,b; Pál et al., 2018) and upregulating their expression genes (Alcázar et al., 2020). APX, SOD and CAT (Nahar et al., 2016; Shah et al., 2020), SOD and CAT (Taie et al., 2019) and CAT (Rady et al., 2019) activities increased in Put-treated plants under Cd stress. On the other hand, Tajti et al. (2018) reported no effect of Put and Spd on the activity of APX and GP under Cd stress. The effects might be related to the differences in concentration and time of treatments and plants and Cd concentration.

In addition to diverse roles of PAs (the most abundant ones: Put, Spm and Spd) in plant metabolism under optimal conditions, the critical roles of PAs linked to tolerance signalling system against stress conditions have been observed (Kumar et al., 2012; Pathak, 2018; Alcázar et al., 2010,2020). The increases in expression levels of several genes related to PA biosynthesis are notable signs of the active defense roles of PA (Alcázar et al., 2020). The relevant increases have been translated to the augmented plant performance. However, the PAs increase could not be generalized to all stresses due to not being simple direct protective molecules but rather as compounds with complex signalling activity and distinguished functions in the stress tolerance (Pál et al., 2015; Tajti et al., 2018). Consequently, the accurate mechanism is largely unknown. Moreover, it is worthy to state that each group of PAs may have different effects. The type of treatments and plant additionally could change their effectiveness (Tajti et al., 2018). Cd stress enhanced Put content and induced the enzymes involved in its synthesis in wheat leaves (Groppa et al., 2007). Similarly, Tajti et al. (2018) reported increases in Put content in leaves and roots under Cd stress and likewise after Put and Spd application under stress. Conversely, Cd stress caused reduced Spd and Spm or had no effect. For instance, Cd decreased root Spd content; Put and Spd application, as seed soaking treatments, had no effect on the content under stress. Similar controversial results were reported for the enzymes involved in PAs degradation like the diamine oxidase (DAO) and polyamine oxidase (PAO) activities under Cd stress and additionally after Put and Spd applications under Cd stress. The gene expression of arginine decarboxylase (ADC) enhanced under Cd stress. Only Spd application, as seed soaking treatment, enhanced the expression under stress. Put and Spd, as treatments, decreased the ADC expression and ornithine decarboxylase (ODC) under the toxicity, additional debatable marks after different PAs application on PAs content under Cd stress (Tajti et al., 2018). Consequently, the type of PAs as well as the type of treatment cause unlike effects that caused different outcomes of the studies. In general, unsettled results led us to a hypothesis that several factors might be involved in PAs content in plants in response to stress condition (e.g., Cd) and since the actual mechanism of PAs protection is not clear, more investigations are needed to have a clear outcome.

Herewith the study, we, for the first time, investigated the effects of putrescine-functionalized carbon quantum dot nanoparticles (Put-CQD NPs) on Cd-stress submitted grapevine seedlings, suggesting the nanoparticles might be considered as a stress-protecting agent. We observed that NPs reduced content of Cd content in leaf and root tissues, in comparison to Cd-stress submitted plants. Along with the reduced uptake of Cd, stress indicators such MDA, H2O2, proline and EL were buffered. We can refer those reductions in values of stress to the further increased activities of APX, SOD and GP. In addition, negative effects of Cd on photosynthetic pigment and chlorophyll fluorescence of the plants were also reduced by the treatments. Consequently, the improvements in physiological and biochemical attributes of the plants were then reflected to increases in fresh and dry weight leaves. Of the assayed concentration, 25 mg L−1 is deemed as the superior concentration due to the similar effectiveness of both NP concentrations tested. Overall, current findings suggest that Put-CQD NPs might be considered as a remarkable and promising stress-protecting agent, as conjugation of Put with CQD was more effective in comparison with the solo, previously recorded uses of Put or CQD. Concerning potential future works, the researches might be addressed on fruit quality since the commercial parts of grapevines are, in general, fruits. The marketing and quality of the fruits are directly linked to the metabolites available. The metabolites might be screened after Put-CDQ NPs applications. A trade-off between yield and quality might be taken into account. Moreover, the recent concern of the researches has been oriented on transgenerational effects/memory of stress and exogenous applications. In this regard, potential epigenetic modifications adapted to improved plant performance might be examined in the future works.

MD, SP, GG, VF and RA supervised and designed the experimental setup. GG and MK performed greenhouse experiments and biochemical studies. GM and HJ synthesized and characterized the NPs. GG and SP analysed data and results, while SP, RA and VF wrote the manuscript. GG and MK prepared the final revised version. All authors read and approved the final manuscript.
